Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition In **VHDL (VHSIC Hardware Description Language)**, the bedrock of every digital system description lies in two pillars: **Data Types** and **Objects**. A *Data Type* defines the **set of values** a signal can take and the **set of operations** that can be applied to those values. An *Object* is a **named holder of a value** belonging to a specific data type, classified into four fundamental classes: **Signals, Variables, Constants, and Files**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** VHDL is a **strongly typed language**. Every object must be explicitly declared with a type, and **type mismatch errors** are caught at compile-time, not run-time. This strict typing prevents a huge class of hardware design bugs before synthesis. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **kitchen pantry system**: - **Data Types** are like the *category of containers* — bottles, jars, tins, boxes. Each container type can only hold specific kinds of ingredients (solid, liquid, powder) and can only undergo specific operations (pour, scoop, shake). - **Objects** are the *labeled containers themselves* placed on shelves. A "Salt Jar" is a **Constant** (it never changes), a "Water Bottle" in your hand is a **Variable** (you can swap it instantly), and a "Water Pipe" connecting the tank to the tap is a **Signal** (it has a flow, delay, and history). > [!NOTE] > **Real-world engineering utility:** Every FPGA bitstream, ASIC netlist, and RTL design begins with these declarations. Misunderstanding types leads to synthesis-simulation mismatches — the **#1 cause of chip re-spins** in industry. ### 1.3 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics | Metric | Value / Standard | |---|---| | **Standard Logic Value `'0'`** | Logic 0 (LOW) | | **Standard Logic Value `'1'`** | Logic 1 (HIGH) | | **`'Z'`** | High-Impedance (tri-stated buffer output) | | **`'U'`** | Uninitialized (simulation only) | | **`'X'`** | Unknown / Conflict (driven by both '0' and '1') | | **`'W'` / `'L'` / `'H'`** | Weak unknown / Weak 0 / Weak 1 | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Hierarchy of VHDL Data Types VHDL data types are organized into **four major categories** defined in the **IEEE Standard 1076**: 1. **Scalar Types** — single, indivisible values 2. **Composite Types** — collections of values 3. **Access Types** — pointers to dynamic objects 4. **File Types** — references to external files Each of these branches into predefined and user-defined subtypes. ### 2.2 Scalar Types — Detailed Breakdown #### (a) Integer Types - Predefined range: `-2,147,483,647` to `+2,147,483,647` (32-bit signed on most simulators). - Used for counters, addresses, parameters. - **Synthesis rule:** Range MUST be bounded for synthesis — unbounded integers are non-synthesizable. ```vhdl signal counter : integer range 0 to 255; -- 8-bit counter ``` #### (b) Real Types - Floating-point; **non-synthesizable** in standard logic. - Used only in **testbench simulation** for analog-like waveforms, delays expressed in time. #### (c) Enumerated Types - User lists all valid values. The cornerstone of hardware description! ```vhdl type state_type is (IDLE, RUN, STOP, ERROR); signal FSM_state : state_type; ``` `std_logic` and `std_logic_vector` (from `IEEE.std_logic_1164`) are themselves predefined **enumerated types** with 9 values: `'U'`, `'X'`, `'0'`, `'1'`, `'Z'`, `'W'`, `'L'`, `'H'`, `'-'`. #### (d) Physical Types - Represent real-world physical quantities. The only predefined physical type is `time`. - `time` is used to specify delays: `wait for 10 ns;` ### 2.3 Composite Types | Subtype | Structure | Synthesis Use | |---|---|---| | **Array** | Homogeneous collection (all same type) | `std_logic_vector`, RAM/ROM models | | **Record** | Heterogeneous collection (mixed types) | CPU instruction packets, bus transactions | ```vhdl type byte_t is array (7 downto 0) of std_logic; -- array type packet_t is record -- record addr : std_logic_vector(3 downto 0); data : std_logic_vector(7 downto 0); valid : std_logic; end record; ``` ### 2.4 Objects — The Four Classes | Object Class | Storage Behavior | Update Mechanism | Scope | Synthesizable? | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Signal** | Holds *projected* output waveform | **Scheduled** — takes effect at end of `process` (delta cycle) | Architecture / Entity | ✅ Yes | | **Variable** | Holds *current* value | **Immediate** — assignment is instant | Process / Subprogram | ✅ Yes | | **Constant** | Fixed at elaboration | Cannot be reassigned | Anywhere | ✅ Yes | | **File** | External file handle | Sequential I/O | Process / Subprogram | ❌ Simulation only | ### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Memorize the following — they are **guaranteed marks** in any KTU exam on this topic. | Concept | Rule / Formula | Notes | |---|---|---| | Signal assignment (concurrent) | `A <= B after 5 ns;` | Inside architecture, outside process | | Signal assignment (sequential) | `A <= B;` | Inside process; **scheduled, not immediate** | | Variable assignment | `A := B;` | Inside process; **immediate, like C** | | Initial value | `signal A : std_logic := '0';` | Runs **once** at elaboration | | Type casting | `std_logic_vector(unsigned(A) + 1);` | Required for arithmetic on vectors | | `std_logic_1164` 9 values | U, X, 0, 1, Z, W, L, H, - | `-` is the **don't-care** value | | Numeric vs Logic | `std_logic` ≠ `bit` | `bit` supports only `'0'`, `'1'` | | Array indexing | `vec(7 downto 0)` is **8 bits** | `vec(0 to 7)` is also 8 bits, different ordering | | Constrained array | `array (0 to 7) of bit` | Fixed size | | Unconstrained array | `array (natural range <>) of bit` | Size set at declaration (e.g., `std_logic_vector`) | > **Real-world engineering utility:** These rules drive **Xilinx Vivado, Intel Quartus, and Synopsys Design Compiler**. Misusing `:=` vs `<=` is the single most common RTL bug in industry — it changes whether you get a flip-flop or a combinational gate. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Signal vs Variable Behavior To fully grasp why signals and variables differ, let us **derive their behavior** in a process using the **delta-cycle** model of VHDL simulation. **Step 1: Setup the VHDL code.** ```vhdl architecture behave of demo is signal sig_a : std_logic := '0'; begin process(sig_a) variable var_a : std_logic := '0'; begin var_a := sig_a; -- (i) variable assignment sig_a <= not var_a; -- (ii) signal assignment end process; end behave; ``` **Step 2: Trace the first delta cycle.** We start with $\text{sig}\_a = 0$ (from initial value). The process triggers. - (i) Variable assignment: $\text{var}\_a := \text{sig}\_a$. Since variable assignment is **immediate**, we have: $$\text{var}\_a^{(t)} = \text{sig}\_a^{(t)} = 0$$ - (ii) Signal assignment: $\text{sig}\_a \leq \text{NOT}(\text{var}\_a)$. The value `1` is **scheduled** to drive `sig_a` at the **end of the current delta cycle**, but `sig_a` itself still reads as `0` *within* this iteration of the process. **Step 3: Trace the second delta cycle.** The signal `sig_a` transitions to `'1'`, triggering the process again (since it's in the sensitivity list). - (i) $\text{var}\_a := \text{sig}\_a = 1$ — so $\text{var}\_a = 1$. - (ii) `sig_a` is scheduled to become $\text{NOT}(1) = 0$. **Step 4: Generalize the toggling pattern.** We see the system **oscillates** between `'0'` and `'1'` on every delta cycle, with **no real time** passing. Therefore: $$\text{sig}\_a(t+\Delta\delta) = \overline{\text{sig}\_a(t)}$$ This produces a **zero-delay oscillator** — a classic VHDL pedagogical example. If we had used `:=` for both lines (i.e., made `sig_a` a variable), the loop would have completed in **one cycle** and the simulation would stop. This is the power of signal vs variable. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering takeaway:** Signals model **wires with capacitance** (waveform propagation), while variables model **local scratchpads** inside a process. Synthesis tools map signals to actual flip-flops or wires, but variables purely to combinational logic *within* the process. ### 3.2 Derivation: Type Conversion for Arithmetic VHDL forbids `std_logic_vector + std_logic_vector` directly. We need **type conversion functions**. **Step 1: Define the problem.** We want to compute $A + B$ where $A, B \in \mathbb{B}^8$ (8-bit binary vectors). **Step 2: Convert to unsigned via the `IEEE.numeric_std` library.** $$\text{unsigned}(A) : \mathbb{B}^8 \rightarrow \mathbb{Z}_{[0, 255]}$$ **Step 3: Perform the addition in integer domain.** $$\text{Result}_{\text{int}} = \text{unsigned}(A) + \text{unsigned}(B)$$ **Step 4: Convert back to logic vector.** $$\text{Result} = \text{std\_logic\_vector}(\text{Result}_{\text{int}})$$ Complete VHDL expression: ```vhdl library IEEE; use IEEE.numeric_std.all; signal A, B, SUM : std_logic_vector(7 downto 0); SUM <= std_logic_vector(unsigned(A) + unsigned(B)); ``` > [!WARNING] > KTU students often write `SUM <= A + B;` directly. This **fails to compile** with `std_logic_1164` — it requires `numeric_std` casting. Examiners will mark this wrong. ### 3.3 Subtype Derivation A **subtype** is a type with optional constraints, inheriting all operators of its base type. $$\text{subtype\_byte is integer range 0 to 255}$$ Here, `subtype_byte` is a subtype of `integer` but restricted. Any operator that works on `integer` works on `subtype_byte` automatically. This is the mathematical basis of why we can write `signal x : std_logic;` (subtype of `std_ulogic` resolved) and still use all logic operators. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 VHDL Type System Hierarchy (Conceptual Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TD root[VHDL Data Types]:::root root --> SCL[Scalar]:::branch root --> CMP[Composite]:::branch root --> ACC[Access]:::branch root --> FIL[File]:::branch SCL --> SC1[Integer]:::leaf SCL --> SC2[Real]:::leaf SCL --> SC3[Enumerated]:::leaf SCL --> SC4[Physical]:::leaf SC3 --> SC3a[std_logic]:::leaf SC3 --> SC3b[bit]:::leaf SC3 --> SC3c[user states]:::leaf CMP --> CM1[Array]:::leaf CMP --> CM2[Record]:::leaf CM1 --> CM1a[std_logic_vector]:::leaf CM1 --> CM1b[bit_vector]:::leaf classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef branch fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#fff classDef leaf fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 ``` ### 4.2 Object Class Architecture (Functional Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR arch[Architecture]:::top --> sig[Signal]:::obj arch --> proc[Process]:::top proc --> var[Variable]:::obj proc --> const[Constant]:::obj proc --> fil[File]:::obj pkg[Package]:::top --> const2[Constant]:::obj pkg --> typ[Type Declaration]:::obj ent[Entity Port]:::top --> sig sig --- s1[Carries waveform
Delta delay]:::desc var --- v1[Immediate value
Like C local]:::desc const --- c1[Frozen at elaboration]:::desc fil --- f1[Simulation I/O only]:::desc classDef top fill:#385723,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef obj fill:#a9d18e,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000 classDef desc fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#666,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 ``` ### 4.3 Signal vs Variable — Delta-Cycle Timing Diagram ```mermaid gantt title Delta-Cycle Behavior of Signal vs Variable dateFormat X axisFormat %s section Signal sig_a Reads old value 0 (cycle 1) :a1, 0, 1 Transitions to 1 (cycle 2) :a2, 1, 1 Reads old value 1 (cycle 2) :a3, 1, 1 Transitions to 0 (cycle 3) :a4, 2, 1 section Variable var_a Assigns 0 immediately (cycle 1) :b1, 0, 1 Assigns 1 immediately (cycle 2) :b2, 1, 1 Assigns 0 immediately (cycle 3) :b3, 2, 1 ``` ### 4.4 Block-Level Flow: Type Declaration to Synthesis ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Type Declaration
type state_t is IDLE, RUN, STOP]:::stepA --> B[Signal Declaration
signal s: state_t := IDLE]:::stepB B --> C[Process Sensitivity
process clk, rst]:::stepC C --> D{Assignment Type}:::dec D -- <= --> E[Concurrent or Scheduled
Synthesizes to Flip-Flop]:::leaf D -- := --> F[Sequential Immediate
Synthesizes to Combinational]:::leaf E --> G[Netlist / Bitstream]:::out F --> G ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Differentiate between Signal and Variable in VHDL.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Signal | Variable | |---|---|---| | Assignment operator | `<=` | `:=` | | Update timing | End of process (delta delay) | Immediate | | Scope | Architecture / Entity | Process / Subprogram | | Synthesis | Sequential element or wire | Pure combinational | | Initialization | Optional, runs at elaboration | Optional, runs on every process entry | Signals model hardware **wires/registers with propagation**, while variables act like **local C variables** for algorithm steps. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]** **List the four classes of objects in VHDL and state which is synthesizable.** **Model Answer:** The four VHDL object classes are: 1. **Signal** – synthesizable (maps to wire / FF). 2. **Variable** – synthesizable (maps to local logic inside process). 3. **Constant** – synthesizable (maps to ROM / hardwired value). 4. **File** – **non-synthesizable** (used only in testbenches for I/O). --- ### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU ESE Module 4 Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the classification of VHDL data types with suitable examples. Show how enumerated types form the basis of `std_logic`. **(b) [7 Marks)** Write a VHDL code that declares an 8-bit `std_logic_vector` signal `DATA` and computes its **byte parity** (XOR of all bits). Use a process and a variable. **Model Solution:** **(a) Classification of VHDL Data Types** *(7 marks)* VHDL defines data types in the **IEEE Standard 1076** library, classified into four major categories: **1. Scalar Types** — represent a single value, no sub-elements. - **Integer**: Discrete whole numbers. Example: `signal cnt : integer range 0 to 15;` - **Real**: Floating-point numbers. **Non-synthesizable**; used in testbenches for analog behavior. - **Enumerated**: User-defined list of named values. Example: ```vhdl type traffic_light is (RED, YELLOW, GREEN); ``` The IEEE 1164 standard defines `std_ulogic` as an enumerated type with 9 values: ```vhdl type std_ulogic is ('U','X','0','1','Z','W','L','H','-'); ``` The resolved subtype `std_logic` then adds bus-resolution logic. So **enumerated types are the foundation** of every multi-value logic representation in VHDL. - **Physical**: Values tied to physical units. The only predefined one is `time`, e.g., `wait for 10 ns;` **2. Composite Types** — collections of values. - **Array**: Homogeneous. Example: `type ram_t is array (0 to 255) of std_logic_vector(7 downto 0);` - **Record**: Heterogeneous. Example: ```vhdl type cpu_instr is record opcode : std_logic_vector(3 downto 0); operand: std_logic_vector(7 downto 0); end record; ``` **3. Access Types** — pointers to dynamic objects; non-synthesizable. **4. File Types** — references to external files for testbench I/O; non-synthesizable. **(b) VHDL Parity Code** *(7 marks)* ```vhdl library IEEE; use IEEE.std_logic_1164.all; entity parity_gen is Port ( DATA : in std_logic_vector(7 downto 0); PAR : out std_logic); end parity_gen; architecture behave of parity_gen is begin process(DATA) variable p : std_logic := '0'; begin p := '0'; for i in 0 to 7 loop p := p xor DATA(i); -- variable immediate assignment end loop; PAR <= p; -- signal assignment at end of process end process; end behave; ``` **Explanation:** - A `variable p` is used so that XOR updates happen **immediately** inside the loop. If we used a signal, parity would not be correct since the signal would only update at the end of the process with the *final* scheduled value. - The signal `PAR` is driven **after** the loop completes. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **delta-cycle mechanism** of VHDL signal assignment. Why does a `<=` assignment not update the signal value immediately? **(b) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between **constrained and unconstrained arrays** with examples. Why is `std_logic_vector` called unconstrained? **Model Solution:** **(a) Delta-Cycle Mechanism** *(7 marks)* In VHDL simulation, **time advances in two granularities**: - **Real time** (e.g., nanoseconds), and - **Delta time (δ)** — an infinitesimal, non-advancing time slice. **Diagram (logic):** ``` Iter 1: process triggers → reads sig_a=0 var_a := 0 (immediate) sig_a <= NOT(0) = 1 (SCHEDULED, not applied yet) ── end of process ── [δ delay] sig_a becomes 1 Iter 2: process triggers (sig_a in sensitivity) → reads sig_a=1 var_a := 1 sig_a <= 0 (scheduled) [δ delay] sig_a becomes 0 ... (oscillates forever) ... ``` **Why not immediate?** Because signals represent **physical wires with delay**. Updating them instantly would collapse the timing model and break the simulation of real hardware (where propagation takes time). The delta-cycle provides a mathematically clean way to **schedule** new values **without** consuming real time, letting simulation iterate until quiescence. **(b) Constrained vs Unconstrained Arrays** *(7 marks)* | Feature | Constrained Array | Unconstrained Array | |---|---|---| | Definition | Range specified at declaration | Range left as `<>` | | Index range | Fixed at type level | Set when object is declared | | Example | `type reg8 is array (7 downto 0) of bit;` | `type bit_vector is array (natural range <>) of bit;` | | Usage | Cannot change size | Size specified per object | ```vhdl -- Constrained: type word8 is array (7 downto 0) of std_logic; signal w : word8; -- automatically 8 bits -- Unconstrained (from IEEE 1164): subtype std_logic_vector is std_ulogic_vector (natural range <>); signal busA : std_logic_vector(15 downto 0); -- 16-bit declared here signal busB : std_logic_vector(7 downto 0); -- 8-bit declared here ``` `std_logic_vector` is called unconstrained because the IEEE 1076.3/1164 standard declares it with an **open range `<>`**. The actual width is only bound when an **object of that type is declared**, giving designers flexibility to reuse the same type for any bus width. --- ### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes that cost marks:** > 1. Using `:=` instead of `<=` for signal assignment — VHDL is **case-sensitive on operators**. 1–2 marks lost. > 2. Forgetting to include `library IEEE; use IEEE.std_logic_1164.all;` — code becomes non-compilable in synthesis tools. 1 mark lost. > 3. Writing `SUM <= A + B;` without `unsigned()` casting. 2 marks lost. > 4. Confusing `bit` (2 values) with `std_logic` (9 values). 1 mark lost. > 5. Declaring an `integer` without `range` — non-synthesizable. 1 mark lost. > 6. Initial value given to a port — **illegal in VHDL**. Use internal signal instead. 1 mark lost. --- ### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Data Types** define *what* values exist; **Objects** define *who* holds them. - Four VHDL **object classes**: Signal, Variable, Constant, File. - Four VHDL **type categories**: Scalar, Composite, Access, File. - **Strong typing** is VHDL's greatest strength — leverage it for bug-free RTL. - `<=` schedules, `:=` executes immediately. - Signals are wires/FFs, variables are local scratchpads. - `std_logic` is a 9-value enumerated subtype; it is **resolved** (multi-driver safe). - `std_logic_vector` is **unconstrained** — width fixed at object declaration. - Use `IEEE.numeric_std` (not `IEEE.std_logic_unsigned`) for arithmetic — this is the **modern KTU-approved approach**. - Always bound `integer` ranges for synthesis. - Files and access types are **non-synthesizable** — testbench only. - Know your 9 `std_logic` values: `U, X, 0, 1, Z, W, L, H, -`. - Subtypes inherit operators of their base type automatically. - **Record types** = heterogeneous; **Array types** = homogeneous.

topics.title: Different Modelling Styles in VHDL - Dataflow
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT416 - DIGITAL SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Different Modelling Styles in VHDL - Dataflow

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Dataflow Modelling** in VHDL (Very High Speed Integrated Circuit Hardware Description Language) is a hardware description style in which the architecture of a digital system is specified using **concurrent signal assignments** that describe the flow of data from inputs to outputs through the application of operators and Boolean expressions. It explicitly captures the *function* and *data transformation* that occurs in a circuit, rather than its structural gate-level connectivity or behavioural sequence of actions.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PEECT416 - Digital System Design**, dataflow modelling sits alongside *structural* and *behavioural* modelling as one of the three fundamental abstraction levels a VHDL designer must master. It is formally implemented inside an `architecture` body using only **concurrent statements**, which execute simultaneously, mirroring the parallel hardware reality of logic gates and arithmetic paths.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Dataflow modelling is essentially a *register-transfer level (RTL)* description. It abstracts the circuit to logical equations and arithmetic operations, making it the natural bridge between **gate-level structural** and **algorithm-level behavioural** descriptions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a **water supply network** in a city:

- **Taps (inputs)** release water.
- **Pipes (signals)** carry the water forward.
- **Junctions, valves, and filters (logic operators like AND, OR, NOT, +, −)** transform the water.
- **Outlet taps (outputs)** deliver the processed water.

The water *flows* continuously, and you only care about **how the input stream is transformed** into the output stream — not about the exact plumbing schedule or which worker turns which valve. This is precisely what dataflow modelling represents in VHDL: you specify *what transformation* is applied to inputs to produce outputs, and VHDL assumes the transformation happens *continuously and concurrently*, just as water flows through all pipes at once.

### 1.3 Key Characteristics of Dataflow Modelling

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Properties of Dataflow Style:**
> 1. Uses **concurrent signal assignment statements** (not `process` blocks).
> 2. Execution order is governed by **data dependencies**, not textual order.
> 3. Best suited for **combinational logic** and **simple arithmetic circuits**.
> 4. Provides a **technology-independent** description (can be synthesized to FPGAs/ASICs).
> 5. Implicitly infers **hardware** such as logic gates, multiplexers, and adders.

### 1.4 Position in the VHDL Modelling Hierarchy

| Modelling Style | Description Level | Typical Construct |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Structural** | Gate / Component level | Component instantiation, `port map` |
| **Dataflow** | Equation / RTL level | Concurrent signal assignments |
| **Behavioural** | Algorithm level | `process`, sequential statements |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Concept of Concurrency

Unlike procedural languages (C, Python) where statements execute sequentially, VHDL's dataflow modelling treats every signal assignment as a **parallel observer**. Each statement continuously monitors its right-hand-side expression and updates the left-hand signal whenever the RHS changes.

The fundamental rule is:

$$
\text{When any signal on the RHS changes} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{LHS signal is re-evaluated}
$$

This is called **event-driven execution** and is the cornerstone of dataflow modelling.

### 2.2 Categories of Dataflow Statements

There are three primary forms of dataflow statements a KTU student must recognize:

#### 2.2.1 Simple (Unconditional) Concurrent Signal Assignment
The most basic form — a direct equation:
```vhdl
Y <= A AND B;
Z <= A + B;
```
Each is a standalone concurrent statement.

#### 2.2.2 Conditional Signal Assignment — `WHEN-ELSE`
This is a **concatenated conditional** form. It looks *like* an if-else but is **concurrent**.

General syntax:
```vhdl
<signal> <= <value1> WHEN <condition1> ELSE
            <value2> WHEN <condition2> ELSE
            <value3>;
```

It is logically equivalent to a **priority multiplexer**, where the first `WHEN` clause has the highest priority.

#### 2.2.3 Selected Signal Assignment — `WITH-SELECT-WHEN`
This is a **selection-based** concurrent form, equivalent to a **case statement** but executed concurrently.

General syntax:
```vhdl
WITH <selector_signal> SELECT
   <signal> <= <value1> WHEN <choice1>,
               <value2> WHEN <choice2>,
               <value3> WHEN OTHERS;
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction for KTU Exams:**
> - `WHEN-ELSE` → priority-based, conditions evaluated in order.
> - `WITH-SELECT-WHEN` → parallel-case, **all choices must be mutually exclusive and exhaustive**; use `WHEN OTHERS` for completeness.

### 2.3 Supported Operators in Dataflow Modelling

| Operator Class | VHDL Operators | Real-World Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Logical** | `AND`, `OR`, `NAND`, `NOR`, `XOR`, `XNOR`, `NOT` | Boolean gates |
| **Relational** | `=`, `/=`, `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=` | Comparators |
| **Arithmetic** | `+`, `−`, `*`, `/`, `MOD`, `REM`, `**` | Adders, multipliers |
| **Shift** | `SLL`, `SRL`, `SLA`, `SRA`, `ROL`, `ROR` | Shift registers |
| **Concatenation** | `&` | Bit-joining |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Construct | Syntax Template | Hardware Inferred | KTU Exam Frequency |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Unconditional assignment | `Y <= A AND B;` | Single AND gate | Very High |
| Conditional (priority) | `Y <= "1" WHEN sel='1' ELSE "0";` | 2:1 MUX with priority | High |
| Selected (parallel) | `WITH sel SELECT Y <= ...;` | 2:1 MUX, parallel | High |
| Arithmetic expression | `S <= A + B + Cin;` | Full adder / ripple adder | High |
| Concatenation | `Y <= A & B;` | Bus joining | Medium |
| Comparison | `Z <= '1' WHEN A > B ELSE '0';` | Magnitude comparator | Medium |

### 2.5 Why Dataflow Matters in Engineering

In real-world chip design, **RTL synthesis tools** (e.g., Synopsys Design Compiler, Xilinx Vivado HLS) accept VHDL dataflow descriptions and automatically synthesize them into optimized **gate-level netlists**. This allows:

1. **Rapid prototyping** of combinational logic at FPGA level.
2. **Technology-independent** design — the same code synthesizes for Spartan-6, Cyclone IV, or ASIC libraries.
3. **Functional verification** through simulation before committing to silicon.
4. **Cleaner, more compact** code than fully structural descriptions.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Tip:** When designing combinational circuits for KTU lab exams, always prefer dataflow over structural for **adders, multiplexers, encoders, decoders, and comparators** — it yields fewer lines of code and avoids component instantiation overhead.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section systematically derives the VHDL dataflow code for canonical combinational circuits frequently asked in KTU exams.

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — 2-to-1 Multiplexer

**Problem Statement:** Design a 2-to-1 multiplexer using dataflow modelling.

**Step 1 — Identify Inputs/Outputs:**
- Inputs: `A`, `B` (data), `S` (select line).
- Output: `Y`.

**Step 2 — Derive Boolean Expression:**
Truth table analysis yields:
$$
Y = \overline{S} \cdot A + S \cdot B
$$

**Step 3 — Translate to VHDL Dataflow:**
```vhdl
library IEEE;
use IEEE.STD_LOGIC_1164.ALL;

entity mux2to1 is
    Port ( A : in  STD_LOGIC;
           B : in  STD_LOGIC;
           S : in  STD_LOGIC;
           Y : out STD_LOGIC);
end mux2to1;

architecture dataflow of mux2to1 is
begin
    Y <= (NOT S AND A) OR (S AND B);
end dataflow;
```

**Step 4 — Reasoning Behind Each Construct:**
- `entity` declares I/O — this is standard for all VHDL designs.
- `architecture dataflow ...` names our chosen modelling style.
- The single concurrent statement directly mirrors the Boolean equation from Step 2.

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — 4-to-1 MUX using `WITH-SELECT-WHEN`

**Step 1 — Boolean Form:**
$$
Y = \overline{S_1}\,\overline{S_0}\,I_0 + \overline{S_1}\,S_0\,I_1 + S_1\,\overline{S_0}\,I_2 + S_1\,S_0\,I_3
$$

**Step 2 — Dataflow Code with Selected Assignment:**
```vhdl
architecture dataflow_mux of mux4to1 is
begin
    WITH sel SELECT
        Y <= I0 WHEN "00",
              I1 WHEN "01",
              I2 WHEN "10",
              I3 WHEN "11";
end dataflow_mux;
```

Here `sel` is a 2-bit `STD_LOGIC_VECTOR`. Note the **comma separators** and absence of `ELSE` — these are mandatory syntactic features of `WITH-SELECT-WHEN`.

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — 4-to-2 Priority Encoder using `WHEN-ELSE`

**Step 1 — Priority Logic:**
If `I(3)='1'`, output `Y = "11"` regardless of others. Otherwise, check `I(2)`, then `I(1)`, then `I(0)`.

**Step 2 — Dataflow Code with Conditional Assignment:**
```vhdl
architecture dataflow_pe of priority_encoder is
begin
    Y <= "11" WHEN I(3) = '1' ELSE
         "10" WHEN I(2) = '1' ELSE
         "01" WHEN I(1) = '1' ELSE
         "00";
end dataflow_pe;
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **order** of `WHEN` clauses is critical: it directly establishes the priority encoding, exactly as a priority encoder's hardware would behave.

### 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Full Adder

**Step 1 — Sum and Carry Equations:**
$$
S = A \oplus B \oplus C_{in}
$$
$$
C_{out} = (A \cdot B) + (C_{in} \cdot (A \oplus B))
$$

**Step 2 — Dataflow Code:**
```vhdl
architecture dataflow_fa of full_adder is
begin
    S    <= A XOR B XOR Cin;
    Cout <= (A AND B) OR (Cin AND (A XOR B));
end dataflow_fa;
```

This two-statement architecture exploits concurrency: both `S` and `Cout` are computed simultaneously, exactly as the hardware adder does.

### 3.5 Worked Example 5 — 4-Bit Ripple Carry Adder (Composite Dataflow)

**Step 1 — VHDL Code:**
```vhdl
architecture dataflow of ripple_adder is
begin
    S    <= A + B + Cin;
    Cout <= '1' WHEN (A + B + Cin) > "1111" ELSE '0';
end dataflow;
```

The expression `A + B + Cin` is a vector addition, directly inferring a 4-bit ripple adder. The carry-out uses `WHEN-ELSE` for conditional flag generation.

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Circuits and Their Dataflow Code

| Circuit | Boolean Expression | Dataflow Construct Used |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| AND gate | $Y = A \cdot B$ | Unconditional assignment |
| 2-to-1 MUX | $Y = \overline{S}A + SB$ | Unconditional with operators |
| 4-to-1 MUX | Selection on 2-bit `sel` | `WITH-SELECT-WHEN` |
| Priority Encoder | Priority order matters | `WHEN-ELSE` ordered |
| Half Adder | $S = A \oplus B$, $C = A \cdot B$ | Unconditional assignments |
| Full Adder | $S = A \oplus B \oplus C_{in}$ | Unconditional + arithmetic |
| 4-bit Comparator | $GT = (A>B)$, $EQ = (A=B)$ | Relational operators |
| Decoder (3-to-8) | One-hot activation | `WITH-SELECT-WHEN` |
| Parity Generator | $P = A \oplus B \oplus C \oplus D$ | XOR reduction |
| BCD to 7-seg | Case-based | `WITH-SELECT-WHEN` |

### 3.7 Delay Modelling in Dataflow (After Clause)

Dataflow also supports **inertial and transport delays**, an advanced KTU concept:

```vhdl
Y <= A AND B AFTER 5 ns;
```

This models **propagation delay** through the inferred gate. After-clauses are essential for **timing simulation** of real hardware behaviour.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Dataflow Modelling — Conceptual Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph inputs [INPUT SIGNALS]
        A[Signal A]
        B[Signal B]
        SEL[Select SEL]
    end

    subgraph logic [CONCURRENT LOGIC LAYER]
        OP1[AND Gate Operator]
        OP2[OR Gate Operator]
        OP3[NOT Operator]
        MUX[Multiplexer Logic]
    end

    subgraph outputs [OUTPUT SIGNALS]
        Y[Signal Y]
        Z[Signal Z]
    end

    A --> OP1
    B --> OP1
    OP1 --> OP2
    SEL --> OP3
    OP3 --> OP2
    OP2 --> Y
    A --> MUX
    B --> MUX
    SEL --> MUX
    MUX --> Z

    classDef inStyle fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3,color:#000
    classDef logicStyle fill:#fff7cc,stroke:#b38f00,color:#000
    classDef outStyle fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
    class A,B,SEL inStyle
    class OP1,OP2,OP3,MUX logicStyle
    class Y,Z outStyle
```

### 4.2 Workflow Diagram — From Specification to Dataflow Code

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Identify Inputs and Outputs] --> S2[Step 2: Derive Boolean Equation or Truth Table]
    S2 --> S3{Step 3: Choose Dataflow Construct}
    S3 --|Unconditional| S4[Use simple assignment Y less than expr]
    S3 --|Conditional Priority| S5[Use WHEN-ELSE construct]
    S3 --|Parallel Selection| S6[Use WITH-SELECT-WHEN construct]
    S4 --> S7[Step 4: Write architecture body]
    S5 --> S7
    S6 --> S7
    S7 --> S8[Step 5: Compile and Simulate]
    S8 --> S9{Step 6: Verify Functionality}
    S9 --|Pass| S10[Step 7: Synthesize to FPGA or ASIC]
    S9 --|Fail| S2

    classDef stepStyle fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b,color:#000
    classDef decisionStyle fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    class S1,S2,S4,S5,S6,S7,S8,S10 stepStyle
    class S3,S9 decisionStyle
```

### 4.3 Comparison — Dataflow vs Behavioural vs Structural

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DF [DATAFLOW MODELLING]
        DF1[Concurrent Signal Assignment]
        DF2[WHEN ELSE Construct]
        DF3[WITH SELECT WHEN Construct]
        DF1 --> DF4[Inferred: Gates and MUXes]
        DF2 --> DF4
        DF3 --> DF4
    end

    subgraph BH [BEHAVIOURAL MODELLING]
        BH1[Process Block]
        BH2[If Else Case Statements]
        BH3[Sequential Statements]
        BH1 --> BH4[Inferred: Registers and FSMs]
        BH2 --> BH4
        BH3 --> BH4
    end

    subgraph ST [STRUCTURAL MODELLING]
        ST1[Component Declaration]
        ST2[Port Map Instantiation]
        ST1 --> ST3[Inferred: Netlist of Components]
        ST2 --> ST3
    end

    classDef dfStyle fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#084298,color:#000
    classDef bhStyle fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#0f5132,color:#000
    classDef stStyle fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#842029,color:#000
    class DF1,DF2,DF3,DF4 dfStyle
    class BH1,BH2,BH3,BH4 bhStyle
    class ST1,ST2,ST3 stStyle
```

### 4.4 Hardware Inference Map — Dataflow Statement to Logic Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Y less than A AND B] --> H1[AND Gate]
    A2[Y less than A OR B] --> H2[OR Gate]
    A3[Y less than NOT A] --> H3[Inverter]
    A4[Y less than A plus B] --> H4[Adder Block]
    A5[Y less than WHEN ELSE chain] --> H5[Priority MUX]
    A6[WITH SELECT WHEN] --> H6[Parallel Case MUX]
    A7[Y less than expr AFTER delay] --> H7[Gate plus Delay Element]

    classDef codeStyle fill:#e2d9f3,stroke:#5a189a,color:#000
    classDef hwStyle fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    class A1,A2,A3,A4,A5,A6,A7 codeStyle
    class H1,H2,H3,H4,H5,H6,H7 hwStyle
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**What is dataflow modelling in VHDL? Mention any two concurrent statements used in it.**

**Model Answer:**

Dataflow modelling is a VHDL description style in which the architecture body uses concurrent signal assignments to specify how data is transformed from inputs to outputs through Boolean or arithmetic expressions. It describes the **flow of data** through operators rather than the structural connectivity or algorithmic behaviour of the circuit.

Two concurrent statements used in dataflow modelling are:
1. **Unconditional concurrent signal assignment** — e.g., `Y <= A AND B;`
2. **Conditional signal assignment** (`WHEN-ELSE`) — e.g., `Y <= "1" WHEN sel='1' ELSE "0";`

*(Alternatively, the selected signal assignment `WITH-SELECT-WHEN` may be cited.)*

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**Differentiate between `WHEN-ELSE` and `WITH-SELECT-WHEN` concurrent statements in VHDL.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | `WHEN-ELSE` | `WITH-SELECT-WHEN` |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Nature** | Conditional / priority-based | Selection / parallel-case based |
| **Evaluation Order** | Sequential priority, top-to-bottom | All choices evaluated simultaneously |
| **Syntax Terminator** | Ends with semicolon `;` after last `ELSE` | Choices separated by commas `,` |
| **Hardware Inferred** | Priority multiplexer | Parallel multiplexer |
| **Requirement** | No need to cover all cases | Must be exhaustive; `WHEN OTHERS` recommended |
| **Example** | `Y <= "1" WHEN s='1' ELSE "0";` | `WITH s SELECT Y <= "1" WHEN '1', "0" WHEN '0';` |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**Question A (14 Marks):**

Design a **4-bit comparator** using dataflow modelling in VHDL. The comparator should output three signals: `EQ` (equal), `GT` (greater than), and `LT` (less than) for two 4-bit inputs `A` and `B`.

**(a)** Write the complete VHDL code using dataflow modelling. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Simulate the design conceptually for the inputs `A = "1010"` and `B = "0110"` and explain the output. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

```vhdl
library IEEE;
use IEEE.STD_LOGIC_1164.ALL;

entity comparator_4bit is
    Port ( A   : in  STD_LOGIC_VECTOR(3 downto 0);
           B   : in  STD_LOGIC_VECTOR(3 downto 0);
           EQ  : out STD_LOGIC;
           GT  : out STD_LOGIC;
           LT  : out STD_LOGIC);
end comparator_4bit;

architecture dataflow of comparator_4bit is
begin
    EQ <= '1' WHEN A = B ELSE '0';
    GT <= '1' WHEN A > B ELSE '0';
    LT <= '1' WHEN A < B ELSE '0';
end dataflow;
```

**Explanation:**
- `EQ` uses the relational operator `=`.
- `GT` uses `>` for magnitude comparison.
- `LT` uses `<`.
- All three assignments are concurrent, mirroring the parallel operation of three comparator units.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

For `A = "1010"` (decimal 10) and `B = "0110"` (decimal 6):

Step 1: Compute `A = B`? → 10 = 6? → **False** → `EQ = '0'`.
Step 2: Compute `A > B`? → 10 > 6? → **True** → `GT = '1'`.
Step 3: Compute `A < B`? → 10 < 6? → **False** → `LT = '0'`.

Expected waveform output:
- `EQ` remains at logic 0.
- `GT` transitions to logic 1.
- `LT` remains at logic 0.

This satisfies the mutually exclusive property of comparator outputs (only one is HIGH at any time).

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**

Design a **BCD to 7-segment decoder** using VHDL dataflow modelling. The decoder takes a 4-bit BCD input `D` and drives a 7-segment display output `SEG` (active low, common anode).

**(a)** Derive the truth table and write the complete VHDL dataflow code. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain how `WITH-SELECT-WHEN` differs from a behavioural `case` statement, even though they appear similar. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

For active-low common-anode display, each segment is lit by `0` and unlit by `1`.

Truth table (segment pattern a-b-c-d-e-f-g for digits 0 to 9):
- 0 → `0000001` (lit: a,b,c,d,e,f)
- 1 → `1001111`
- 2 → `0010010`
- 3 → `0000110`
- 4 → `1001100`
- 5 → `0100100`
- 6 → `0100000`
- 7 → `0001111`
- 8 → `0000000`
- 9 → `0000100`

VHDL Code:
```vhdl
library IEEE;
use IEEE.STD_LOGIC_1164.ALL;

entity bcd_to_7seg is
    Port ( D   : in  STD_LOGIC_VECTOR(3 downto 0);
           SEG : out STD_LOGIC_VECTOR(6 downto 0));
end bcd_to_7seg;

architecture dataflow of bcd_to_7seg is
begin
    WITH D SELECT
        SEG <= "0000001" WHEN "0000",
               "1001111" WHEN "0001",
               "0010010" WHEN "0010",
               "0000110" WHEN "0011",
               "1001100" WHEN "0100",
               "0100100" WHEN "0101",
               "0100000" WHEN "0110",
               "0001111" WHEN "0111",
               "0000000" WHEN "1000",
               "0000100" WHEN "1001",
               "1111111" WHEN OTHERS;
end dataflow;
```

`"1111111"` blanks the display for invalid BCD inputs (10–15).

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

| Aspect | `WITH-SELECT-WHEN` (Dataflow) | `case ... when` (Behavioural) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Location** | Outside any process (concurrent) | Must be inside a `process` |
| **Execution** | Truly concurrent — runs continuously | Sequential within a process |
| **Synthesizability** | Synthesizes to a parallel MUX | Synthesizes to MUX or sequential logic (if inside clocked process) |
| **Hardware Implication** | Combinational only | Can be combinational or sequential depending on sensitivity list |

Even though the syntax resembles `case`, `WITH-SELECT-WHEN` is fundamentally a **concurrent** construct and is the dataflow equivalent of the behavioural `case`.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make — Leading to Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Semicolon vs Comma Confusion:** Using `;` instead of `,` between choices in `WITH-SELECT-WHEN` is a **syntax error** worth 2–3 marks loss.
> 2. **Missing `WHEN OTHERS`:** In `WITH-SELECT-WHEN`, failure to provide `WHEN OTHERS` for exhaustive coverage leads to **inferred latches** during synthesis — examiners explicitly check this for 2 marks.
> 3. **Wrong Priority Order in `WHEN-ELSE`:** Many students write priority encoder code with the lowest priority condition first, breaking the design — **2 marks deducted**.
> 4. **Using `STD_LOGIC` with `'1'` and `'0'` vs `STD_LOGIC_VECTOR` with `" "`:** Mixing bit literal styles causes compile errors — **1–2 marks loss**.
> 5. **Forgetting `library IEEE;` and `use IEEE.STD_LOGIC_1164.ALL;`** — instant mark loss.
> 6. **Confusing `=` (assignment in sequential) with `<=` (assignment in concurrent):** Using single `=` in dataflow code is the **most frequent error**, costing 2 marks.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Dataflow Modelling in VHDL:**
>
> - **Definition:** Dataflow modelling describes circuits through **concurrent signal assignments** representing data transformation.
> - **No `process` block:** All statements are concurrent and execute in parallel.
> - **Three Forms:**
>   * Unconditional: `Y <= A AND B;`
>   * Conditional: `WHEN-ELSE` (priority MUX)
>   * Selected: `WITH-SELECT-WHEN` (parallel MUX)
> - **Operators:** Logical, Arithmetic, Relational, Shift, Concatenation.
> - **Best For:** Combinational circuits — adders, MUXes, encoders, decoders, comparators, parity generators.
> - **Delay Modelling:** Use `AFTER <time>` clause for propagation delay simulation.
> - **Bit Literals:** `'1'` for single bit; `"01"` for vector.
> - **Hardware Inference:** Synthesizes to gates, MUXes, adders — *not* to registers unless clocked `process` is involved.
> - **Position in Syllabus:** Sits between structural (gate-level) and behavioural (algorithmic) abstraction levels.
> - **Always include `library IEEE;` and `use IEEE.STD_LOGIC_1164.ALL;`** at the top.
> - **Remember `WHEN OTHERS`** in `WITH-SELECT-WHEN` for exhaustiveness.
> - **Syntax delimiters:** `,` separates choices in `WITH-SELECT-WHEN`; `;` terminates a complete concurrent statement.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Behavioural and Structural Modelling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT416 - DIGITAL SYSTEM DESIGN > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Behavioural and Structural Modelling # MODULE 4 : BEHAVIOURAL AND STRUCTUCTURAL MODELLING ## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ### 1.1 Formal Definition of HDL Modeling Styles In **Hardware Description Languages (HDL)** such as **VHDL** and **Verilog**, every digital circuit can be described using three primary abstraction levels (modeling styles). The two styles mandated for this module are: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Behavioural Modelling** describes a digital system by specifying **what the circuit does** (its algorithm/function) rather than **how it is built** from gates. It is the **highest level of abstraction** and uses sequential programming constructs inside a `process` block to model the response of the circuit to input changes. > > **Structural Modelling** describes a digital system by explicitly **interconnecting predefined components** (gates, flip-flops, or lower-level modules) using **component instantiation** and **port mapping**. It is essentially a **netlist** – a textual schematic that mirrors a hardware connection diagram. The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly demands that students master both styles because: 1. **Behavioural** style is used for high-level algorithmic design (FSMs, controllers, CPUs). 2. **Structural** style is used for **hierarchical design**, library-based composition, and synthesis-friendly gate-level description. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Recipe vs The Assembly Line" Imagine you are asked to bake a cake: | Approach | Real-world Action | HDL Equivalent | |---|---|---| | **Behavioural** | "Cream butter and sugar until fluffy. Add eggs one at a time. Bake at 180 °C for 30 minutes." | Algorithm-based, describes *what* happens in each step regardless of components. | | **Structural** | "Take tray #A, place the pre-mixed batter from bowl #B, connect oven #C using cable #D, switch on the main socket #E." | Connection-based, lists every *part* and *wire*. | > [!NOTE] > In a real **FPGA/ASIC** design flow, you write *behavioural* code for clarity and simulation speed, then let the synthesis tool *infer* the lower-level structure (latches, LUTs) automatically. **Structural** code, on the other hand, is what a synthesized netlist looks like – a giant bill-of-materials of components and wires. ### 1.3 Hierarchy of VHDL Modeling Styles ``` ┌──────────────────────────┐ │ System / Algorithm │ ← Highest abstraction ├──────────────────────────┤ │ BEHAVIOURAL MODELLING │ ← Focus of Module 4 (Part A) ├──────────────────────────┤ │ Dataflow Modelling │ ← Concurrent assignments ├──────────────────────────┤ │ STRUCTURAL MODELLING │ ← Focus of Module 4 (Part B) ├──────────────────────────┤ │ Gate / Switch Level │ ← Lowest abstraction └──────────────────────────┘ ``` ### 1.4 Why Two Styles Coexist — The Design Philosophy > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why not use only one style?** > - Behavioural code is **concise, readable, and fast to write**, but synthesis results can sometimes be unpredictable (e.g., unintended latches). > - Structural code is **verbose but predictable** — every gate and wire is visible, making it ideal for **technology mapping, area estimation, and timing closure** in industry. > - A real KTU/industry project uses **both**: behavioural for top-level RTL, structural for standard cell instantiation. # 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD CHEAT SHEET ## 2.1 STRUCTURAL MODELLING — Theoretical Foundation ### 2.1.1 Core Concept Structural modelling treats a digital system as a **collection of components and signals**. It is the closest textual representation of a circuit schematic. The keyword used in VHDL is the **COMPONENT** declaration followed by **PORT MAP**. ### 2.1.2 The Three Mandatory Building Blocks | Block | Purpose | Required Syntax | |---|---|---| | **1. Entity Declaration** | Defines the I/O ports of the *top-level* design | `entity NAME is port(...); end entity;` | | **2. Architecture (Structural)** | Declares lower-level components and internal signals | `architecture STR of NAME is … begin … end architecture;` | | **3. Component Declaration** | Tells the architecture what the sub-modules look like | `component CMP_NAME is port(...); end component;` | | **4. Component Instantiation** | Creates actual instances and maps signals | `INST_NAME: CMP_NAME port map (A, B, Y);` | | **5. Signal Declaration** | Wires that connect components | `signal W: std_logic;` | ### 2.1.3 Port Mapping Modes | Mode | Keyword | Direction | Typical Use | |---|---|---|---| | **In** | `in` | Into the entity | Inputs, clocks, control | | **Out** | `out` | Out of the entity | Outputs (cannot be read internally) | | **Inout** | `inout` | Bidirectional | Bidirectional buses, I/O pads | | **Buffer** | `buffer` | Out but readable | Internal feedback | ### 2.1.4 Two Port-Mapping Styles > [!IMPORTANT] > **Positional Mapping** – ports are mapped by *order*. Compact but error-prone. > ```vhdl > U1: AND2 port map (A, B, Y); > ``` > **Named Mapping** – ports are mapped by *name*. Self-documenting and KTU-preferred. > ```vhdl > U1: AND2 port map (A => A, B => B, Y => Y); > ``` --- ## 2.2 BEHAVIOURAL MODELLING — Theoretical Foundation ### 2.2.1 Core Concept Behavioural modelling describes a circuit as a **set of responses to events**. The central construct is the **`process`** statement, which is a *sequential* block of code that runs in *parallel* with other concurrent statements in the architecture. ### 2.2.2 The PROCESS Statement Anatomy ```vhdl process_label : process (sensitivity_list) -- variable declarations begin -- sequential statements end process; ``` - **Sensitivity list** – the set of signals that wake up the process when they change. - **WAIT FOR / WAIT UNTIL / WAIT ON** – alternative ways to suspend execution. - **IF-THEN-ELSE / CASE / LOOP** – high-level decision-making. ### 2.2.3 The Golden Rule of Process > [!WARNING] > Inside a process, statements execute **sequentially**, but the process itself is a **concurrent statement** with respect to the rest of the architecture. Multiple processes run in *parallel*, exactly as multiple hardware blocks operate concurrently. ### 2.2.4 Sensitivity List vs Wait Statement — Mutual Exclusion > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Theorem 4.1:** A process must use **EITHER** a sensitivity list **OR** `wait` statements, but **NEVER both**. Using both causes a simulator error because the process would never be re-triggered. ### 2.2.5 Sequential Statements Used in Behavioural Modelling | Statement | Function | Example | |---|---|---| | **Variable assignment** `:=` | Local, immediate | `tmp := A and B;` | | **Signal assignment** `<=` | Scheduled, after delta delay | `Y <= A and B;` | | **If-THEN-ELSIF-ELSE** | Priority encoder of conditions | MUX, decoders | | **CASE-WHEN** | Multi-way branch | ALU, FSM, decoders | | **FOR-LOOP** | Iterative hardware | ripple adder, parallel prefix | | **WHILE-LOOP** | Test-before iteration | testbench stimulus | | **WAIT UNTIL clk'event and clk='1'** | Clock-edge detection | flip-flops | | **ASSERT / REPORT** | Simulation messages | self-checking testbenches | ### 2.2.6 Latch Inference — The Hidden Trap > [!WARNING] > If a process assigns to a signal in some but not all branches of an `IF` or `CASE`, the synthesizer **infers a level-sensitive latch** to hold the previous value. This is the #1 source of KTU mark loss in behavioural modelling questions. --- ## 2.3 KTU FORMULA SHEET — Concise Cheat Sheet | Construct | VHDL Syntax | When it Models | |---|---|---| | Component Declaration | `component NAME port(...); end component;` | Pre-synthesis module prototype | | Component Instantiation | `INST: NAME port map (...);` | Hardware sub-block | | Positional Port Map | `U1: AND2 port map (A, B, Y);` | Compact binding | | Named Port Map | `U1: AND2 port map (A=>A, B=>B, Y=>Y);` | Safe binding | | Generic Map | `U1: AND2 generic map (N => 4) port map (...);` | Parameterized width | | Process with Sensitivity | `process (A, B, SEL) begin … end process;` | Combinational logic | | Process with WAIT | `process begin wait until clk='1'; … end process;` | Clocked logic | | Edge Detection | `if clk'event and clk='1' then` | Rising-edge D-FF | | `rising_edge(clk)` | Standard alternative to `clk'event and clk='1'` | Cleaner syntax | | Concurrent Assignment | `Y <= A and B after 5 ns;` | Dataflow (not behavioural) | | Signal vs Variable | `Y <= A;` (signal) `tmp := A;` (variable) | Timing semantics differ | | Latch Inference | Missing assignment in branch | **Avoid in combinational** | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Behavioural Modelling** is used to write **Register-Transfer Level (RTL)** code in **Intel Quartus, Xilinx Vivado, Synopsys Design Compiler**. - **Structural Modelling** is used to instantiate **IP cores**, **standard cells from a vendor library**, and to compose **hierarchical ASIC/FPGA designs** used in CPUs, GPUs, and SoCs. - The **GDSII** final layout is essentially a fully structural netlist expanded down to transistors. # 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL EXAMPLES ## 3.1 EXAMPLE 1 — 2-to-1 Multiplexer (Behavioural Modelling) ### 3.1.1 Problem Statement Design a **2:1 MUX** with inputs `A`, `B`, select `SEL`, and output `Y`. Use **behavioural** style. ### 3.1.2 Truth Table (Spec) | SEL | A | B | Y | |---|---|---|---| | 0 | x | x | A | | 1 | x | x | B | ### 3.1.3 Boolean Function (Theoretical Base) $$ Y = (\overline{SEL} \cdot A) + (SEL \cdot B) $$ ### 3.1.4 Behavioural VHDL Code (Step-by-Step Derivation) **Step 1** — Declare the *entity* with four ports. ```vhdl library IEEE; use IEEE.STD_LOGIC_1164.ALL; entity mux2_1_behav is port ( A, B, SEL : in STD_LOGIC; Y : out STD_LOGIC ); end mux2_1_behav; ``` **Step 2** — Declare the *architecture* of type **behavioural**. ```vhdl architecture BEHAV of mux2_1_behav is begin process (A, B, SEL) -- sensitivity list begin if SEL = '1' then Y <= B; else Y <= A; end if; end process; end BEHAV; ``` > [!NOTE] > **Step 3 — Synthesis Result** (mental model): The synthesizer recognizes the IF structure, then infers the **two AND gates + one OR gate** shown in the dataflow equation. The behavioural code is therefore functionally equivalent to the gate-level MUX, but written 4× more concisely. --- ## 3.2 EXAMPLE 2 — 2-to-1 MUX (Structural Modelling) ### 3.2.1 Architecture (Component Declaration + Instantiation) ```vhdl architecture STRUCT of mux2_1_struct is component AND2 port ( I1, I2 : in STD_LOGIC; O : out STD_LOGIC ); end component; component OR2 port ( I1, I2 : in STD_LOGIC; O : out STD_LOGIC ); end component; component NOT1 port ( I : in STD_LOGIC; O : out STD_LOGIC ); end component; signal nSEL, T1, T2 : STD_LOGIC; begin U1 : NOT1 port map (SEL, nSEL); U2 : AND2 port map (A, nSEL, T1); U3 : AND2 port map (B, SEL, T2); U4 : OR2 port map (T1, T2, Y); end STRUCT; ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Derivation Walkthrough**: > - **Line `U1`**: Generates $\overline{SEL}$ → stored in internal signal `nSEL`. > - **Line `U2`**: Generates $\overline{SEL} \cdot A$ → stored in `T1`. > - **Line `U3`**: Generates $SEL \cdot B$ → stored in `T2`. > - **Line `U4`**: OR-combines the two terms → produces final $Y$. --- ## 3.3 EXAMPLE 3 — Full Adder (Both Styles Side by Side) A full adder has equations: $$ S = A \oplus B \oplus C_{in} $$ $$ C_{out} = (A \cdot B) + (C_{in} \cdot (A \oplus B)) $$ ### 3.3.1 Behavioural Model (Single Process, CASE) ```vhdl library IEEE; use IEEE.STD_LOGIC_1164.ALL; entity full_adder_behav is port ( A, B, Cin : in STD_LOGIC; S, Cout : out STD_LOGIC ); end full_adder_behav; architecture BEHAV of full_adder_behav is begin process (A, B, Cin) begin case std_logic_vector'(A & B & Cin) is when "000" => S <= '0'; Cout <= '0'; when "001" => S <= '1'; Cout <= '0'; when "010" => S <= '1'; Cout <= '0'; when "011" => S <= '0'; Cout <= '1'; when "100" => S <= '1'; Cout <= '0'; when "101" => S <= '0'; Cout <= '1'; when "110" => S <= '0'; Cout <= '1'; when others=> S <= '1'; Cout <= '1'; end case; end process; end BEHAV; ``` ### 3.3.2 Structural Model (Two Half-Adders + One OR Gate) ```vhdl architecture STRUCT of full_adder_struct is component half_adder port ( X, Y : in STD_LOGIC; S, C : out STD_LOGIC ); end component; component OR2 port ( I1, I2 : in STD_LOGIC; O : out STD_LOGIC ); end component; signal S1, C1, C2 : STD_LOGIC; begin HA1 : half_adder port map (A, B, S1, C1); HA2 : half_adder port map (S1, Cin, S, C2); U1 : OR2 port map (C1, C2, Cout); end STRUCT; ``` > [!NOTE] > The structural version *physically realises* the **ripple equation**: $$ C_{out} = (A \cdot B) + C_{in} \cdot (A \oplus B) $$ > Each `half_adder` instance becomes a physical half-adder block in the final silicon — perfect for **hierarchical synthesis**. --- ## 3.4 EXAMPLE 4 — D Flip-Flop with Asynchronous Reset (Behavioural, Clocked) ```vhdl library IEEE; use IEEE.STD_LOGIC_1164.ALL; entity dff_async_reset is port ( D, CLK, RST : in STD_LOGIC; Q : out STD_LOGIC ); end dff_async_reset; architecture BEHAV of dff_async_reset is begin process (CLK, RST) -- both in sensitivity list begin if RST = '1' then Q <= '0'; elsif rising_edge(CLK) then Q <= D; end if; end process; end BEHAV; ``` ### 3.4.1 Derivation of the Implied Hardware > [!IMPORTANT] > **Logic Flow**: > 1. `RST='1'` → process assigns `Q='0'` *asynchronously* (no clock edge needed). > 2. `RST='0'` and **rising edge of CLK** → `Q` captures the value of `D`. > 3. Synthesizer infers: **one D-FF + one asynchronous clear input**. > 4. Note: putting `RST` in the sensitivity list is *mandatory* for asynchronous reset; otherwise the FF would be synchronous. --- ## 3.5 EXAMPLE 5 — 4-Bit Ripple Carry Adder (Structural, Generic) ```vhdl library IEEE; use IEEE.STD_LOGIC_1164.ALL; use IEEE.NUMERIC_STD.ALL; entity rca_4bit is generic ( N : integer := 4 ); port ( A, B : in UNSIGNED(N-1 downto 0); Cin : in STD_LOGIC; Sum : out UNSIGNED(N-1 downto 0); Cout : out STD_LOGIC ); end rca_4bit; architecture STRUCT of rca_4bit is component full_adder port ( A, B, Cin : in STD_LOGIC; S, Cout : out STD_LOGIC ); end component; signal carry : STD_LOGIC_VECTOR(N downto 0); begin carry(0) <= Cin; gen_add: for i in 0 to N-1 generate FAi : full_adder port map ( A => A(i), B => B(i), Cin => carry(i), S => Sum(i), Cout=> carry(i+1) ); end generate; Cout <= carry(N); end STRUCT; ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key VHDL-2008 Feature** — `for ... generate` loop is the structural equivalent of *arrayed hardware*. Each `FAi` instance is a physical full-adder slice; **changing `N` re-instantiates N full-adders automatically**. This is *structural* modelling, but loop-based and parameterised — a KTU favourite. # 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Hierarchical Structural View of a Full Adder ```mermaid graph TD A[Input A] --> HA1_IN1 B[Input B] --> HA1_IN2 Cin[Input Cin] --> HA2_IN2 HA1_IN1[HA1.X] --> HA1 HA1_IN2[HA1.Y] --> HA1 HA1[Half Adder HA1] --> HA1_S[S1] HA1[Half Adder HA1] --> HA1_C[C1] HA1_S[S1 Signal] --> HA2_IN1 HA2_IN1[HA2.X] --> HA2 HA2_IN2[HA2.Y] --> HA2 HA2[Half Adder HA2] --> S_OUT[Output S] HA2[Half Adder HA2] --> C2[C2] HA1_C[C1 Signal] --> OR_IN1 C2[C2 Signal] --> OR_IN2 OR_IN1[OR.I1] --> OR1 OR_IN2[OR.I2] --> OR1 OR1[OR Gate] --> COUT[Output Cout] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Process Execution Flow in Behavioural Modelling ```mermaid flowchart TD START([Simulation Start]) --> WAIT[Process Suspended] WAIT --> EVENT{Event on
sensitivity list} EVENT -- Yes --> EXEC[Execute
Sequential Code] EVENT -- No --> WAIT EXEC --> ASSIGN[Schedule Signal
Assignments] ASSIGN --> WAIT EXEC --> ENDLOOP{Last Statement?} ENDLOOP -- No --> EXEC ENDLOOP -- Yes --> WAIT ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — 4-Bit RCA Structural Expansion ```mermaid graph LR A0[A0] --> FA0 B0[B0] --> FA0 C0[Cin] --> FA0 FA0[FA0] --> S0[S0] FA0 --> C1 A1[A1] --> FA1 B1[B1] --> FA1 C1 --> FA1 FA1[FA1] --> S1[S1] FA1 --> C2 A2[A2] --> FA2 B2[B2] --> FA2 C2 --> FA2 FA2[FA2] --> S2[S2] FA2 --> C3 A3[A3] --> FA3 B3[B3] --> FA3 C3 --> FA3 FA3[FA3] --> S3[S3] FA3 --> COUT[Cout] ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Synthesis Path: Behavioural → Structural ```mermaid flowchart LR BEHAV[VHDL Behavioural Code
Process + IF/CASE] --> SYN[Synthesis Tool
Quartus / Vivado / DC] SYN --> RTL[RTL Netlist
Boolean equations] RTL --> GATEMAP[Technology Mapping] GATEMAP --> STRUCT[Structural Netlist
Gates + Wires] STRUCT --> PLACE[Place and Route] PLACE --> CHIP[FPGA Bitstream / ASIC GDSII] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram**: The synthesis tool **automatically converts** behavioural RTL into the same structural netlist you would have written by hand. This is why a senior engineer can verify synthesis by writing a *behavioural* testbench and a *structural* design — they are the two faces of the same circuit. # 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** > **Differentiate between Behavioural and Structural modelling styles in VHDL with suitable examples.** #### Model Answer (3 Marks): | Aspect | Behavioural | Structural | |---|---|---| | **Abstraction** | High – describes *what* circuit does | Low – describes *how* circuit is wired | | **Key Construct** | `process`, `if`, `case` | `component`, `port map` | | **Style** | Algorithm-like | Netlist-like | | **Example** | `if SEL='1' then Y <= B; else Y <= A;` | `U1: AND2 port map (A, nSEL, T1);` | | **Synthesis** | Auto-infers hardware | Explicitly maps to cells | Behavioural is **concise**; structural is **explicit and hierarchical**. (1 mark each for definition + 1 mark for the example/table). --- ### Question A2 > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** > **What is the significance of a sensitivity list in a VHDL process? What happens if a signal is missed from the sensitivity list?** #### Model Answer (3 Marks): - The **sensitivity list** (2 marks) determines which signal transitions will trigger (wake up) the process for execution. It models the asynchronous response of combinational logic to input changes. - If a signal used inside the process is **omitted** from the sensitivity list (1 mark), the process will not re-execute when that signal changes, leading to **stale output values** and **simulation mismatches with synthesis**. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and provides internal choice (Or option), as per KTU 2024 ESE pattern. --- ### Question B1(A) — 14 Marks > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]** > **(a)** Explain the structural modelling style in VHDL with the general syntax for **component declaration** and **component instantiation**. Illustrate by writing a complete VHDL code for a **2:1 multiplexer** using only AND, OR and NOT gates. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Write the VHDL behavioural code for the same **2:1 multiplexer** using an `IF-ELSE` construct inside a process. Compare the two styles in terms of code length, readability, and synthesis predictability. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Structural Model** (7 marks) ```vhdl library IEEE; use IEEE.STD_LOGIC_1164.ALL; entity mux2_1_struct is port ( A, B, SEL : in STD_LOGIC; Y : out STD_LOGIC ); end mux2_1_struct; architecture STRUCT of mux2_1_struct is component AND2 port ( I1, I2 : in STD_LOGIC; O : out STD_LOGIC ); end component; component OR2 port ( I1, I2 : in STD_LOGIC; O : out STD_LOGIC ); end component; component NOT1 port ( I : in STD_LOGIC; O : out STD_LOGIC ); end component; signal nSEL, T1, T2 : STD_LOGIC; begin U1 : NOT1 port map (SEL, nSEL); U2 : AND2 port map (A, nSEL, T1); U3 : AND2 port map (B, SEL, T2); U4 : OR2 port map (T1, T2, Y); end STRUCT; ``` - **Explanation (3 marks)**: Each instance `U1…U4` corresponds to one physical gate. Internal signals `nSEL, T1, T2` act as connecting wires. This is a literal text-based schematic. **(b) Behavioural Model** (7 marks) ```vhdl library IEEE; use IEEE.STD_LOGIC_1164.ALL; entity mux2_1_behav is port ( A, B, SEL : in STD_LOGIC; Y : out STD_LOGIC ); end mux2_1_behav; architecture BEHAV of mux2_1_behav is begin process (A, B, SEL) begin if SEL = '1' then Y <= B; else Y <= A; end if; end process; end BEHAV; ``` - **Comparison Table (3 marks)**: | Aspect | Structural | Behavioural | |---|---|---| | Code length | 4 instances + 3 components | 4-line process | | Readability | Schematic-like | Algorithm-like | | Synthesis | Predictable, uses explicit cells | Tool-inferred, may differ | | Hierarchy | Easy composition from primitives | Single-level abstraction | --- ### Question B1(B) — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]** > **(a)** Design a **4-bit ripple carry adder** using **structural modelling**. Your code should instantiate a previously declared `full_adder` component four times using a `generate` loop. Show the entity, architecture, and explain how the `carry` signal array chains the four adders. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Rewrite the same 4-bit adder using **behavioural modelling** with a single process and a `for` loop over the bit indices. Discuss the conditions under which a designer would prefer one style over the other. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Structural Code with `generate`** (7 marks) ```vhdl library IEEE; use IEEE.STD_LOGIC_1164.ALL; use IEEE.NUMERIC_STD.ALL; entity rca_4bit is port ( A, B : in UNSIGNED(3 downto 0); Cin : in STD_LOGIC; Sum : out UNSIGNED(3 downto 0); Cout : out STD_LOGIC ); end rca_4bit; architecture STRUCT of rca_4bit is component full_adder port ( A, B, Cin : in STD_LOGIC; S, Cout : out STD_LOGIC ); end component; signal carry : STD_LOGIC_VECTOR(4 downto 0); begin carry(0) <= Cin; GEN_ADDS : for i in 0 to 3 generate FAI : full_adder port map ( A => A(i), B => B(i), Cin => carry(i), S => Sum(i), Cout=> carry(i+1) ); end generate; Cout <= carry(4); end STRUCT; ``` **Explanation** (3 marks): The `generate` loop creates **four physical full-adder slices** `FA0…FA3`. The `carry` signal vector of length 5 acts as a *ripple chain*: `carry(0)=Cin`, `carry(4)=Cout`. **(b) Behavioural Code** (7 marks) ```vhdl library IEEE; use IEEE.STD_LOGIC_1164.ALL; use IEEE.NUMERIC_STD.ALL; entity rca_4bit_behav is port ( A, B : in UNSIGNED(3 downto 0); Cin : in STD_LOGIC; Sum : out UNSIGNED(3 downto 0); Cout : out STD_LOGIC ); end rca_4bit_behav; architecture BEHAV of rca_4bit_behav is begin process (A, B, Cin) variable c : STD_LOGIC; begin c := Cin; for i in 0 to 3 loop Sum(i) <= A(i) xor B(i) xor c; c := (A(i) and B(i)) or (c and (A(i) xor B(i))); end loop; Cout <= c; end process; end BEHAV; ``` **Comparison** (3 marks): - **Behavioural** is shorter and portable across tools; useful for simulation. - **Structural** is preferred for **technology-specific libraries** and **FPGA primitive instantiation**, where each `full_adder` maps to a known hardware macro. - For **ASIC design**, structural is mandatory; for **rapid FPGA prototyping**, behavioural is preferred. --- ## 5.3 KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks** > > 1. **Mixing sensitivity list with `wait` statements** – strictly forbidden; KTU deducts **2 marks** flat. > 2. **Forgetting `end process;`** – causes a syntax error, **0 marks** for that part. > 3. **Missing `end generate;`** inside the `for ... generate` block – common in 4-bit adder questions. > 4. **Wrong case in VHDL keywords** (`Process` instead of `process`) – some simulators are case-insensitive, but **Xilinx Vivado** will reject the file. > 5. **Latch inference** – not assigning `Y` in all `if/case` branches results in a *level-sensitive latch*, which loses **timing and marks**. > 6. **Forgetting `signal` declaration** for internal wires in structural code – components will not connect. > 7. **Using `OUT` port for internal read** – the correct mode for feedback is `BUFFER`, not `OUT`. Marks lost: **1–2**. --- ## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Behavioural & Structural Modelling** > > ✅ **Three VHDL modelling styles**: Behavioural, Dataflow, Structural. Module 4 covers two of them. > > ✅ **Structural** = **Component declaration + Component instantiation + Port map**. Each instance becomes one physical hardware block. > > ✅ **Behavioural** = **Process + IF/CASE/LOOP**. The process is a concurrent statement but its *body* executes sequentially. > > ✅ **Sensitivity list** = the trigger set for combinational behavioural logic. **Must include every signal read inside the process.** > > ✅ **`wait until` clauses** = alternative to sensitivity list. **Never combine the two.** > > ✅ **Latch inference rule**: every branch of `if/case` must assign to the output, OR a default value must precede the `if`. > > ✅ **Positional vs Named port map**: positional is compact, named is safe; KTU prefers named for clarity. > > ✅ **Generic map** allows parameterised component widths (e.g., `N=8` for 8-bit adder). > > ✅ **`generate` loop** is the structural way to instantiate an *array* of identical cells. > > ✅ **Edge detection** = `rising_edge(clk)` or `clk'event and clk='1'`. Mandatory for clocked (sequential) logic. > > ✅ **Real-world bridge**: behavioural = RTL (Register Transfer Level), structural = gate-level netlist. Synthesis tools auto-convert one into the other. > > ✅ **Hierarchy** is the strength of structural modelling — sub-modules declared once, instantiated many times.

topics.title: VHDL Constructs and Codes for Combinational and Sequential Circuits
topics.content:

## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is VHDL?

**VHDL** stands for **VHSIC Hardware Description Language**, where VHSIC itself means **Very High Speed Integrated Circuit**. It is a formal, standardized, IEEE-standardized language (IEEE 1076) used to describe, model, simulate, and synthesize digital hardware systems at multiple levels of abstraction — from algorithmic behavior down to gate-level netlists.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *VHDL is a hardware description language used to model the behavior and structure of digital systems at various levels of abstraction, enabling both simulation and synthesis of combinational and sequential circuits.*

In the context of KTU's PEECT416 course, VHDL is the **primary tool** for implementing digital designs on **PLDs (Programmable Logic Devices)** such as CPLDs and FPGAs, and for targeting **ASIC (Application-Specific Integrated Circuit)** design flows.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: VHDL as a "Recipe for a Circuit"

Imagine you are a chef writing a recipe to bake a cake. The recipe does not tell the *physical* atoms of flour, sugar, and eggs how to arrange themselves. Instead, it gives **precise instructions** (inputs, steps, conditions, expected outputs) to a baker who knows how to physically combine them.

Similarly, **VHDL does not directly manufacture transistors**. It provides:
- **Inputs** → declared as `IN` ports (`A`, `B`, `SEL`)
- **Internal logic / behavior** → written in **processes** or **concurrent assignments**
- **Outputs** → declared as `OUT` ports (`Y`, `CARRY`)

A **synthesis tool (compiler)** then "bakes" this code into a real hardware netlist of gates, flip-flops, and interconnects.

| Recipe Term | VHDL Equivalent |
|-------------|-----------------|
| Ingredients | Signals / Ports |
| Cooking Steps | Process / Concurrent Statements |
| Final Dish | Synthesized Hardware |
| Tasting | Simulation |

---

### 1.3 VHDL Modeling Levels (Abstraction Hierarchy)

VHDL supports **three primary levels of abstraction**, each with a different trade-off between detail and readability:

1. **Behavioral Modeling** (Highest abstraction)
   - Describes *what* the circuit does, not *how*.
   - Uses `process` blocks, `if-then-else`, `case`, `wait`.
   - Closest to a high-level algorithm.

2. **Dataflow Modeling** (Mid abstraction)
   - Describes the *flow of data* through the circuit using concurrent signal assignments.
   - Uses `when`, `with-select`, `<=`.

3. **Structural Modeling** (Lowest abstraction)
   - Describes the *gate-level interconnection* of components.
   - Uses `component instantiation` and `port maps`.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** In KTU board exams, students are expected to write VHDL code in **all three styles** for the same circuit, demonstrating mastery of modeling flexibility.

---

### 1.4 Why VHDL for KTU B.Tech?

- **Standardization:** IEEE 1076 standard — portable across vendors (Xilinx, Intel/Altera, Lattice).
- **Hierarchy:** Supports modular design (top-down approach).
- **Reusability:** Components can be instantiated multiple times.
- **Simulation & Synthesis:** Same code can be both simulated and synthesized — a feature unique to HDLs.
- **Industry relevance:** Used in 80% of FPGA/ASIC design flows worldwide.

---

### 1.5 Physical/System Metrics Relevant to VHDL Designs

- **Propagation Delay ($t_{pd}$):** Time for an output to change after an input change. Measured in **nanoseconds (ns)** or **picoseconds (ps)**.
- **Setup Time ($t_{su}$) and Hold Time ($t_{h}$):** Critical for sequential elements (flip-flops).
- **Clock Frequency ($f_{clk}$):** Maximum operating speed, measured in **MHz** or **GHz**.
- **Resource Utilization:** Number of **LUTs (Look-Up Tables)**, **flip-flops**, and **slices** consumed on an FPGA.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These metrics are outputs of the **synthesis report**, not values coded by the designer. KTU exams focus on writing correct, synthesizable VHDL — not on timing closure.

---
